problem_statement: The document examines whether human review (a peer or supervisor evaluating a worker's output) increases meaningful human engagement with AI-generated content, and finds that outcome-focused review instead makes workers offload to AI, accepting its output with little modification.
magnitude_evidence: "Two-thirds of people do not modify the AI-generated content (27)"; workers "are spending two more hours on average trying to fix the low-quality AI-generated content they encountered at work (10)"
insufficiency_evidence: "participants who believed that their work would be reviewed by another person submitted a post that was more similar to the AI-generated content (M = 0.82) compared to participants in the No Review condition (M = 0.75), t(239) = 2.38, p = 0.018" — review paradoxically reduced modification across seven experiments (N=2,895)
notes: Domain is AI-generated knowledge-work content (social media posts, postcards, lesson plans), not AI-generated code specifically; relevance is by analogy to human oversight of AI output. Coding is mentioned only as an example use of AI.
